prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 About me: I'm a 42-year-old man.
 I read online that Bell's palsy almost always goes away completely on its own within a few weeks. My cousin had it and didn't take any medication and was fine.
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
 - History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.
 - Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 72, Temp 37.0°C
 - Exam: Right-sided facial weakness affecting both the forehead and lower face. I can raise my eyebrows weakly on the right. Eye doesn't fully close. No other neurological deficits noted.
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Glucose: 94 mg/dL (70-100) normal
 - HbA1c: 5.4% (4.0-5.6) normal
 - WBC: 7.2 K/uL (4.0-11.0) normal
 - ESR: 12 mm/hr (0-22) normal
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D – choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy, which is most consistent with Bell’s palsy, often after a recent viral illness. Your vitals and basic labs don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even when Bell’s palsy improves on its own, early treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) started within about 72 hours can improve the chance of full recovery, so timing matters. The eye that won’t fully close needs protection (lubricating drops/ointment and taping at night) to prevent corneal injury. Stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other neurologic deficits, but new facial paralysis still warrants prompt in-person assessment.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, inability to fully close the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy, which is most consistent with Bell’s palsy—often after a recent viral illness. Your vitals and labs don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even though many people recover, early treatment (typically steroids, sometimes antivirals) started within about 72 hours can improve the chance of full recovery. The incomplete eye closure also needs prompt eye protection (lubricating drops/ointment and taping at night) to prevent corneal injury. Stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other deficits, but new facial weakness still warrants urgent medical evaluation.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam describe sudden, one-sided facial weakness involving both the forehead and lower face, with inability to fully close the eye and no other neurologic problems. That pattern fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy) more than a typical stroke, because strokes usually spare the forehead. Your labs are normal and don’t point to infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even if Bell’s palsy often improves, early evaluation matters because treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) is most effective when started within about 72 hours, and eye protection is important to prevent corneal injury since your eye doesn’t close fully. A clinician should also confirm there aren’t other causes that need different treatment.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam show weakness of the entire right side of the face, including the forehead, which most often fits a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), sometimes after a recent viral illness. Your normal vitals and lack of arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or other neurologic findings make stroke less likely, though not impossible. Bell’s palsy often improves, but the timing matters: starting treatment (usually steroids, sometimes antivirals) within about 72 hours can improve the chance of full recovery. The biggest immediate issue is eye protection because you can’t fully close the right eye, which can lead to corneal injury; you may need lubricating drops/ointment and possibly taping the eye shut at night. Your labs don’t suggest diabetes or infection/inflammation.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, inability to fully close the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit best with a peripheral facial nerve palsy, commonly Bell’s palsy, often following a viral illness. Your vitals and labs don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or major inflammation. Even if Bell’s palsy is likely, it’s important to be evaluated promptly because early treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) is time-sensitive and can improve the chance of full recovery. Also, protecting the eye that won’t close is urgent to prevent corneal injury (lubricating drops/ointment, taping at night). While stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other deficits, new facial weakness still warrants prompt medical assessment.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 82%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, inability to fully close the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy such as Bell’s palsy, often after a viral illness. The normal labs don’t point to infection, diabetes, or inflammation as a cause. Even though many cases improve, early evaluation matters because treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) is most effective when started within about 72 hours of onset, and you need eye-protection guidance to prevent corneal injury since your eye doesn’t close fully. A stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other deficits, but new facial weakness still warrants prompt medical assessment.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (sudden one-sided facial weakness involving both the forehead and lower face, with inability to fully close the eye and no other neurologic problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy, which is most consistent with Bell’s palsy, often after a recent viral illness. Your normal vitals and labs don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation as a trigger. Even though many cases improve, early treatment (usually a short course of steroids, sometimes antivirals) started within about 72 hours can improve the chance of full recovery. The eye not closing fully is important because it can dry out and be injured; you may need lubrication/taping and an exam to protect the cornea. Stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other deficits, but new facial paralysis should still be assessed promptly.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (one-sided facial weakness including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy, commonly Bell’s palsy, often after a viral illness. Your vitals and labs don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or systemic inflammation. Even if Bell’s palsy is likely, new facial paralysis should be assessed promptly because stroke and other causes must be considered clinically, and treatment for Bell’s palsy (especially steroids) is most effective when started early (ideally within 72 hours). Eye protection is important because incomplete eyelid closure can injure the cornea.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (sudden one-sided facial weakness involving both the forehead and lower face, with inability to fully close the eye, and no other neurologic problems) fit best with a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy such as Bell’s palsy, often following a viral illness. Your vitals and labs don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or a systemic inflammatory condition. Even though many people recover, early treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) started within about 72 hours can improve the chance and speed of full recovery. Also, not being able to close the eye can lead to corneal injury, so you may need urgent eye-protection measures. Stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other deficits, but new facial weakness should still be assessed promptly.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

Your vitals and basic labs don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or an inflammatory condition. Even though many people recover, early evaluation matters because treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) is most effective when started within ~72 hours, and your eye needs protection if it won’t close fully to prevent corneal injury. A clinician should also confirm there aren’t red flags for other causes.  
TRIAGE: C
